project.title: Developer Profiles, Work Contexts, and AI Tool Adoption
project.context: Generative AI tools are now part of many software development workflows, but adoption and confidence vary by developer profile, role, industry, organization size, and work arrangement. This project turns the Stack Overflow Developer Survey 2025 into a guided D3 exploration for comparing adoption, trust, perceived limits, frustrations, human-help needs, and AI-agent readiness across developer cohorts.
project.high_level_goal: Help technical decision-makers identify which developer groups adopt AI tools, where trust or confidence gaps remain, and where training, review practices, or policy support may be needed.

target_users.name: Technical team managers, training leads, technology decision-makers, and software development professionals
constraints: Need quick comparisons without reading a 172-variable survey table
Understand software-development and AI vocabulary but have limited time
Use the site in Chrome on a standard screen
Need visible denominators, missing-data caveats, and accessible encodings
target_users.use_context: Use the standalone website to compare developer cohorts, discuss AI-tool adoption strategy, identify training needs, and reason about where human review remains important.

dataset.source: Stack Overflow Developer Survey 2025, collected through a self-administered online survey from May 29 to June 23, 2025 and distributed primarily through Stack Overflow channels. The project subset is derived from results.csv and schema.csv.
dataset.grain: One row represents one survey respondent.
known_limits: Voluntary survey responses are descriptive and not representative of all developers worldwide
Respondents are connected to the Stack Overflow ecosystem
AI-related questions have substantial non-response and NA values
Multi-select fields use semicolon-separated values and should not be interpreted as categories that sum to 100 percent
Associations are descriptive and should not be presented as causal effects

variables.name: ResponseId
variables.typical_range: Unique respondent ids
variables.notes: Technical key only; not used as a visual measure.

variables.name: Age
variables.typical_range: Survey age bands plus missing markers

variables.name: EdLevel
variables.typical_range: Formal education levels plus missing markers
variables.notes: Used for sentiment and trust comparisons.

variables.name: YearsCode
variables.typical_range: Coding experience values plus special text categories
variables.notes: May need bucketing for readable cohort comparisons.

variables.name: WorkExp
variables.typical_range: Professional experience values plus missing markers

variables.name: DevType
variables.typical_range: Developer role categories
variables.notes: Primary cohort field for role-based comparisons.

variables.name: OrgSize
variables.typical_range: Organization size categories plus missing markers

variables.name: ICorPM
variables.typical_range: Individual contributor, people manager, or missing markers

variables.name: RemoteWork
variables.typical_range: Remote, hybrid, in-person, and missing markers

variables.name: Industry
variables.typical_range: Industry categories plus missing markers
variables.notes: Used for adoption and trust comparisons.

variables.name: AISelect
variables.typical_range: Daily, weekly, monthly or infrequently, plan to use, do not plan to use, missing markers

variables.name: AISent
variables.typical_range: Favorable to unfavorable attitude categories plus missing markers

variables.name: AIAcc
variables.typical_range: Agreement or confidence categories plus missing markers

variables.name: AIComplex
variables.typical_range: Agreement or confidence categories plus missing markers
variables.notes: Used to derive complex-task confidence rates.

variables.name: AIThreat
variables.typical_range: Threat perception responses plus missing markers

variables.name: AIFrustration
variables.typical_range: Frustration categories plus missing markers
variables.notes: Multi-select; percentages use valid respondent denominators and do not sum to 100 percent.

variables.name: AIAgents
variables.typical_range: Agent use frequency categories plus missing markers

variables.name: AIAgentChange
variables.typical_range: Perceived change categories plus missing markers
variables.notes: Paired with AIAgents for agent-readiness section.

variables.name: AIHuman
variables.typical_range: Human-help situations plus missing markers
variables.notes: Multi-select; percentages use valid respondent denominators and do not sum to 100 percent.

variables.name: SOFriction
variables.typical_range: Friction frequency responses plus missing markers

variables.name: baseline_difference
variables.notes: Cohort rate minus overall survey baseline.

variables.name: missing_n
variables.notes: Missing or NA count for each rate.

questions.question: Do developer roles and industries that use AI tools also trust their accuracy or complex-task performance?
questions.why_it_matters: Managers need to know where high adoption still requires review practices, training, or policy support.

questions.question: How is AI adoption distributed across profile and work-context groups?
questions.why_it_matters: Experience and manager/contributor splits show clearer adoption patterns than RemoteWork or OrgSize, so the first version prioritizes YearsCode, Age, Role, and ICorPM.

questions.question: Which cohorts differ most from the overall survey baseline on adoption, trust, confidence, and job-threat perception?
questions.why_it_matters: A compact diagnostic view helps users spot cohorts that need attention without scanning many separate charts.

questions.question: Which AI frustrations dominate, and how do they vary by adoption group or selected cohort?
questions.why_it_matters: Frustration categories translate broad caution into concrete training and workflow issues.

questions.question: Where do developers still prefer human help, and how does this vary by trust or confidence group?
questions.why_it_matters: The project should show that adoption does not eliminate the need for human judgment.

questions.question: Which profiles use AI agents, and is agent use associated with perceived change in development work?
questions.why_it_matters: Agent readiness is useful for deciding whether teams are ready for more autonomous AI workflows.

visualizations.title: Adoption-Trust Gap By Cohort
visualizations.question: q_v1
visualizations.task: Compare two percentage rates for the same cohort and rank cohorts by the gap
visualizations.takeaway: High-adoption cohorts may still have lower trust or complex-task confidence, revealing where governance or review support is needed.

encoding.x: Percent on a shared 0 to 100 scale
encoding.y: Cohort category such as DevType or Industry
encoding.color: Two color-blind-safe hues for adoption and trust or confidence
encoding.text: Direct percentage labels, valid n, and missing n in detail view

interaction.purpose: Switch grouping variable and switch the second dot between trust and complex-task confidence
interaction.state_visible: Active grouping and metric labels remain visible above the chart
interaction.reset_available: Reset button returns to DevType and trust metric

alternatives_considered.why_rejected: It makes the gap less visually direct and adds extra ink for the same two-rate comparison.

risks.misinterpretation: Users may read a gap as causal evidence that adoption lowers trust.
risks.prevention: Label results as descriptive associations and show valid denominators for both rates.

visualizations.title: AI Adoption Composition Across Profiles And Work Contexts
visualizations.question: q_v2
visualizations.task: Compare the full composition of ordered AISelect categories across groups
visualizations.takeaway: Groups differ not only in whether they use AI, but in the balance between daily, weekly, occasional, planned, and non-use.

encoding.color: Ordered AISelect categories using a color-blind-safe categorical palette
encoding.text: Percent labels when space permits plus detail panel for count and denominator
encoding.facets: Tabs for years of coding experience, age, role, and manager versus contributor status

interaction.purpose: Switch profile or work-context tab, sort unordered categories, and pin a cohort
interaction.state_visible: Active tab, sort mode, and pinned cohort are visibly indicated
interaction.reset_available: Reset button clears pinned cohort and restores default sort

alternatives_considered.why_rejected: It hides the difference between daily, weekly, occasional, planned, and non-use categories.

risks.misinterpretation: Users may compare tiny groups as if they were equally reliable.
risks.prevention: Show valid n, missing n, and de-emphasize RemoteWork and OrgSize because analysis found low visual separation.

visualizations.title: AI-Agent Readiness By Cohort
visualizations.question: q_v6
visualizations.task: Compare AI-agent use with perceived work change for the same cohort
visualizations.takeaway: AI-agent readiness is a core adoption story: AI/ML engineers, founders, senior executives, and managers show high agent use and stronger reported work change.

encoding.x: Percent on a shared 0 to 100 scale
encoding.color: Two color-blind-safe hues for agent use and perceived work change
encoding.shape: Paired dots with optional connecting segment
encoding.text: Direct percentage labels and valid denominators

interaction.purpose: Switch grouping field and reveal valid denominators for both agent metrics
interaction.state_visible: Active grouping field and low-n warnings are displayed

alternatives_considered.chart_type: Scatterplot of agent use versus work change
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Scatterplots are less consistent with V1 and make row-by-row cohort comparison harder for this guided story.

risks.misinterpretation: Users may trust agent findings even when response counts are low.
risks.prevention: Hide or de-emphasize cohorts below the low-n threshold and show missing counts prominently.

visualizations.title: Perception Differences From The Survey Baseline
visualizations.question: q_v3
visualizations.task: Compare each cohort against the overall baseline across several metrics
visualizations.takeaway: Cohorts that look average on adoption may still stand out on trust, complex-task confidence, sentiment, or job-threat concern.

encoding.color: Diverging color scale centered on zero percentage-point difference from baseline
encoding.text: Signed percentage-point labels and valid n in detail view

interaction.purpose: Change grouping variable, reorder rows by selected metric, and inspect cell denominators
interaction.state_visible: Selected metric, grouping variable, and row order are shown above the matrix
interaction.reset_available: Reset button restores default selected cohorts and metric order

alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Separate charts make cross-metric cohort comparison slower and consume too much vertical space.

risks.misinterpretation: Users may overread small percentage-point differences.
risks.prevention: Use muted color near zero, show exact differences, and surface valid n for each cell.

visualizations.title: AI Frustrations By Adoption Group
visualizations.question: q_v4
visualizations.task: Compare how top multi-select frustration percentages change from occasional to daily AI users
visualizations.takeaway: The dominant frustration, almost-right AI output, increases with heavier AI use, so mature adopters need review and debugging practices rather than only onboarding.

encoding.x: Percent of valid AIFrustration respondents on a visible 0 to 85 percent axis
encoding.y: Frustration category, one horizontal row per category
encoding.color: Adoption group or pinned cohort with redundant card headings
encoding.shape: Lines connect occasional, weekly, and daily user points within each frustration row

interaction.purpose: Hover or keyboard focus reveals exact value, valid denominator, and multi-select caveat for each point
interaction.state_visible: No persistent filter state; values and the percent axis remain visible in the static view
interaction.reset_available: No persistent state is changed, so moving focus away returns to the default view

alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Multi-select categories do not sum to 100 percent, so stacked bars would imply a false part-to-whole relationship.

risks.misinterpretation: Users may assume multi-select frustration shares sum to 100 percent.
risks.prevention: Label the field as multi-select and state that each percentage uses the valid respondent denominator.

visualizations.title: Human-Help Situations By Trust Group
visualizations.question: q_v5
visualizations.task: Compare multi-select human-help situations across trust, confidence, or adoption groups
visualizations.takeaway: Even groups that adopt AI tools still prefer human help for specific tasks where judgment, accountability, or context matters.

encoding.color: Sequential color scale for percent of valid respondents
encoding.text: Direct percentage labels with count and denominator in detail view

interaction.purpose: Switch column grouping between trust, complex-task confidence, and adoption status
interaction.state_visible: Active grouping and multi-select denominator are displayed
interaction.reset_available: Reset button returns to AIAcc trust grouping

alternatives_considered.chart_type: Grouped bars for every human-help situation
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Grouped bars would be too wide and harder to scan for cross-group patterns.

risks.misinterpretation: Users may treat human-help categories as mutually exclusive.
risks.prevention: Label AIHuman as multi-select and avoid stacked part-to-whole encodings.
